title: Neural tube defect: failure of primary neurulation
vignette: A 35-year-old woman, gravida 2 para 0, is seen in the clinic for an early anatomy ultrasound at 18 weeks after she was noted to have an elevated alpha fetoprotein on prenatal screening. Ultrasound shows a complex cystic lesion overlying the lumbosacral spine, ventriculomegaly, and hindbrain herniation. These abnormalities are most likely due to failure of which of the following processes?

clues.note: Elevated maternal serum AFP signals an open neural tube defect: AFP leaks from exposed fetal neural tissue into amniotic fluid and maternal blood. This single lab points straight at a neural tube closure failure.

clues.text: complex cystic lesion overlying the lumbosacral spine
clues.note: A cystic sac over the lumbosacral spine is a myelomeningocele — herniation of meninges and neural tissue through an unfused vertebral arch. This is the structural hallmark of failed posterior neuropore closure.

Ventriculomegaly + downward hindbrain herniation = Chiari II (Arnold-Chiari) malformation, which is tethered to the myelomeningocele. The open spinal defect pulls the hindbrain caudally, obstructing CSF and causing hydrocephalus.

whatMatters: Elevated AFP + a cystic lumbosacral sac + Chiari II (ventriculomegaly/hindbrain herniation) = an open neural tube defect (myelomeningocele). These all stem from failure of PRIMARY NEURULATION — incomplete closure of the neural tube (posterior neuropore) by week 4 — answer E.
strategy: When AFP is up and a spinal cystic defect + Chiari II appear, classify the embryologic step: the neural TUBE itself fails to close = primary neurulation. Don't jump to neural CREST (those cells form PNS/melanocytes/etc.) — crest migration failure gives different syndromes, not a myelomeningocele.

options.text: Neuronal migration within the cerebral cortex

eliminations.label: primitive streak regression — gives sacrococcygeal teratoma
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the primitive streak normally regresses by week 4; persistent remnants are pluripotent. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: failed regression causes a sacrococcygeal teratoma (a germ-cell tumor), not an open spinal sac with Chiari II. ✅ Correct if: imaging showed a heterogeneous mass with mixed tissue types at the coccyx.

eliminations.label: cerebellar vermis aplasia — Dandy-Walker, not this
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the cerebellar vermis forms the midline cerebellum; its agenesis causes Dandy-Walker malformation (large posterior fossa, 4th-ventricle cyst). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Dandy-Walker is a posterior-fossa cyst, not a lumbosacral sac with elevated AFP; the hindbrain herniation here is Chiari II tied to a spinal NTD. ✅ Correct if: the cyst were in the posterior fossa with an absent vermis and no spinal defect.

eliminations.label: neural crest migration — wrong cell population (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: neural crest cells migrate to form PNS ganglia, Schwann cells, melanocytes, adrenal medulla, craniofacial bones, and more. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: their failure causes things like Hirschsprung disease, Waardenburg, or DiGeorge — NOT closure of the neural tube; a myelomeningocele is the TUBE failing to close, which is neurulation, not crest migration. ✅ Correct if: the defect were aganglionic colon, pigment/hearing anomalies, or conotruncal cardiac/parathyroid defects.

eliminations.label: cortical neuronal migration — gives lissencephaly
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: neurons migrate radially to build the six-layer cortex during weeks 12-24. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: migration defects cause lissencephaly/heterotopias (smooth brain, seizures), not an open lumbosacral defect with raised AFP. ✅ Correct if: imaging showed a smooth (agyric) cortex or gray-matter heterotopia.

reasoning: Elevated AFP → open neural tube defect
Ventriculomegaly + hindbrain herniation = Chiari II (tethered to the NTD)
Open tube = neural tube failed to close → failure of primary neurulation → answer E
mechanism: During primary neurulation (weeks 3-4) the neural plate folds and fuses into the neural tube; the posterior neuropore normally closes by day ~28. Failure of fusion leaves an open spinal defect (spina bifida → myelomeningocele) that leaks AFP and acetylcholinesterase into amniotic fluid. The associated Chiari II malformation (caudal hindbrain herniation) obstructs CSF flow, producing ventriculomegaly/hydrocephalus. Folate deficiency is the major modifiable risk factor.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Embryology > Nervous system: primary neurulation and neuropore closure, neural tube defects (spina bifida occulta/meningocele/myelomeningocele), Chiari II + hydrocephalus, AFP & acetylcholinesterase markers, folate prevention. Contrast neural crest derivatives table.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Translate the findings to the failed STEP, not just the diagnosis: open spinal sac + Chiari II + high AFP = the neural TUBE didn't close (neurulation). Map each distractor to the structure it would actually damage.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on neurulation, neuropore, myelomeningocele, Chiari II, ventriculomegaly, primitive streak, alpha fetoprotein? Tell me which and I'll define it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Neural crest (C) is the classic trap because 'neural' is in the name. Crest = PNS/melanocyte/adrenal medulla derivatives; the TUBE closing is neurulation. Keep crest vs tube straight.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The teaching figure shows neural tube formation (neural plate → folds → neural crest pinches off → tube closes). Practice identifying the neural groove, crest, surface ectoderm, and notochord so you can pinpoint which structure failed.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Missed as C → build one flashcard contrasting neural TUBE (neurulation → NTDs) vs neural CREST (migration → Hirschsprung/Waardenburg/DiGeorge).

keywords.front: Elevated maternal AFP + spinal cystic sac
Embryologic step that failed in spina bifida
Ventriculomegaly + hindbrain herniation with a spinal NTD
Confirmatory amniotic-fluid marker for open NTD

keywords.back: PNS ganglia, Schwann cells, melanocytes, adrenal medulla, craniofacial

firstAid.detail: Embryology > CNS: primary neurulation, spina bifida spectrum, myelomeningocele, Chiari II, AFP/AChE markers, folate prevention.
firstAid.location: Embryology > CNS: primary neurulation, spina bifida spectrum, myelomeningocele, Chiari II, AFP/AChE markers, folate prevention.

firstAid.detail: Embryology: primitive streak persistence (sacrococcygeal teratoma), vermis agenesis (Dandy-Walker), neural crest migration disorders, cortical migration (lissencephaly).
firstAid.location: Embryology: primitive streak persistence (sacrococcygeal teratoma), vermis agenesis (Dandy-Walker), neural crest migration disorders, cortical migration (lissencephaly).

firstAid.detail: Embryology: full neural crest list — important to separate from neural tube so you don't mix the two failure modes.
firstAid.location: Embryology: full neural crest list — important to separate from neural tube so you don't mix the two failure modes.

memoryHook: Open tube → AFP escapes → sac on the spine + Chiari II. The TUBE failing to close is NEURULATION (not crest).
